drilldownInstructions: 1. Check billing model: on-demand ($6.25/TB scanned) vs flat-rate/editions (committed slots). 2. description: OpenAI charges per million tokens processed, with separate rates for input and output. Output tokens cost 3-4x more than input tokens. Key pricing (per 1M tokens): GPT-4o: $2.50 input / $10.00 output. GPT-4o-mini: $0.15 input / $0.60 output (17x cheaper than GPT-4o). GPT-4.1: $2.00 input / $8.00 output. GPT-4.1-mini: $0.40 input / $1.60 output. GPT-4.1-nano: $0.10 input / $0.40 output. o3-mini: $1.10 input / $4.40 output. Cached input tokens get a 50% discount. The Batch API gives 50% off all prices for non-urgent jobs processed within 24 hours. One token is approximately 4 characters or 0.75 English words. A typical API request with a 500-word prompt uses about 670 input tokens.

description: Standard tier routes traffic via ISP networks at lower cost than Premium tier's Google backbone. Use Standard for bulk data transfers, backups, and non-user-facing egress to save 20-40%.
title: Use Standard network tier for non-latency-critical egress

description: OpenAI charges vary dramatically by model. gpt-4o-mini costs ~$0.15/M input tokens vs gpt-4o at $2.50/M (17x more expensive). Use gpt-4o-mini for basic tasks (classification, summarization, simple Q&A) and route to gpt-4o only for complex reasoning. Check your Cost by Model chart in Observe > OpenAI to identify which models consume the most budget and whether they can be downgraded.
automatedCheck: openai:/v1/organization/costs?group_by=line_item → model breakdown. If expensive models (gpt-4o, gpt-4) account for >50% of spend, recommend routing simple tasks to gpt-4o-mini.
title: Use tiered models — route to cheaper models for simple tasks

description: Output tokens cost 3-4x more than input tokens. Without a max_tokens limit, the model generates unnecessary text that inflates your bill. Set a strict, realistic max_tokens limit on every API request. For example: classification tasks need ~10 tokens, summaries ~200, detailed answers ~500. Check your output-to-input token ratio in the Token Usage chart — if output tokens are disproportionately high, you're likely not capping responses.
automatedCheck: openai:/v1/organization/usage/completions?group_by=model → compare input_tokens vs output_tokens ratio per model. High output/input ratio (>0.5) suggests missing max_tokens limits.
title: Cap output token length (max_tokens)

description: In chat applications, the full conversation history is sent with every request as input tokens. A 20-message conversation can accumulate 10K+ tokens per turn. Only include the last 3-5 messages or use a summarized context. This directly reduces your input token costs which are billed on every single API call.
automatedCheck: openai:/v1/organization/usage/completions → if avg input_tokens per request > 5000 (calculated as total_input_tokens / num_model_requests), chat history is likely not being truncated.
title: Truncate chat history to reduce input tokens

description: OpenAI automatically caches prompt prefixes over 1024 tokens, giving you a 50% discount on cached input tokens. To maximize caching: put static content (system prompts, tool definitions, few-shot examples) at the BEGINNING of the messages array, and keep it identical across requests. Check your cached_tokens ratio — if input_cached_tokens is low relative to total input, you're not benefiting from caching.
automatedCheck: openai:/v1/organization/usage/completions → input_cached_tokens / input_tokens ratio. If < 20%, prompt structure can be optimized for better caching.
title: Leverage prompt caching for static system prompts

description: OpenAI's Batch API processes requests asynchronously within 24 hours at a 50% discount. Use it for: bulk data labeling, document summarization, content classification, embedding generation, and any task that doesn't need real-time responses. Simply submit requests to /v1/batches instead of direct endpoints.
automatedCheck: openai:/v1/organization/usage/completions → if num_model_requests > 1000/day and batch field is null/empty for most requests, recommend migrating non-urgent tasks to Batch API.
title: Use the Batch API for non-urgent tasks (50% discount)

description: A fine-tuned gpt-4o-mini can match or outperform gpt-4o for domain-specific tasks, at 17x lower inference cost. If you're using few-shot examples (eating input tokens) or complex system prompts to get gpt-4o to behave correctly, consider fine-tuning gpt-4o-mini instead. Fine-tuning has a one-time training cost but dramatically reduces ongoing inference costs.
automatedCheck: openai:/v1/organization/costs → if gpt-4o or gpt-4 costs > $200/month with consistent use patterns, fine-tuning a smaller model is likely cost-effective.
title: Fine-tune smaller models instead of complex prompting on larger ones

description: System prompts are billed as input tokens on EVERY single API turn. A 2000-token system prompt costs you tokens on every request. Audit your system prompts: remove repetitive instructions, consolidate rules, and use concise language. A 50% reduction in system prompt length = 50% savings on that portion of input costs across all requests.
automatedCheck: Manual review. If average input_tokens > 2000 and output_tokens < 500, system prompt is likely dominating input costs.
title: Optimize system prompts — keep them concise

description: High-resolution image inputs are tokenized as multiple 512x512 tiles, consuming hundreds of tokens per image. If your vision task doesn't require fine detail (e.g., general classification, scene description), set detail:'low' for a flat token rate regardless of image size. This can reduce vision costs by 80%+ for large images.
automatedCheck: openai:/v1/organization/usage/completions → input_image_tokens. If > 0 and growing, review whether high-detail is needed for all image tasks.
title: Set image detail to 'low' for vision tasks when possible
